details.text: Derivative: الْوَاقِعَةُ from وَقَعَ is the occurrence that happens. It is the فَاعِلَة form which can be used in Arabic for singular incidents. It is said that the تأنيث (making feminine) of the فَاعِل form with a Taa’ Marbootah (فاعلَة) is used to imply abundance. In this case it implies a huge, major or tremendous event (الْوَاقِعَةُ).

Derivative: وَقَعَتِ – وَقَعَ means for something to occur, happen or take place. It also means to fall or to drop. Something that happens is like something dropping or falling down upon something or someone i.e., an occurrence, calamity or event.

إِذَا وَقَعَتِ الْوَاقِعَةُ

- When the past tense (وَقَعَتِ) is preceded by “when” (إِذَا) it changes the meaning to the future.

وَالسَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ

- This is unique in that the Mubtada’ (Subject) and Khabar (information about the subject) are the same: “And السَّابِقُونَ (the first and foremost) are the السَّابِقُونَ (the first and foremost)”. مُسابِق (Musaabiq) refers to someone who participates in the race or contest whilst the سَابِق (Saabiq) is the one who has come out in front and already won the race or contest.

Derivative: الْمُقَرَّبُونَ – مُقَرَّب (Muqarrab) comes from تَقْرِيب (Taqreeb) which means to bring something closer and closer. To bring something close in one go is called مُقْرَب (Muqrab) whilst to bring something closer gradually or little by little is called مُقَرَّب (Muqarrab).

أُولَٰئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ

- Usually, this statement would grammatically include هُم (Hum – they (are)) between the أُولَٰئِكَ and الْمُقَرَّبُونَ i.e., أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ. For purposes of بَلَاغَة (eloquence, rhetoric and impact) theهم (Hum) is omitted as if to say الْمُقَرَّبُونَ (those who have been brought near or close) are so close that they do not require the هم (Hum) to even be stated.
Derivative: الْأَوَّلِينَ can refer to those who came first and can also mean those who ranked first.
Derivative: ثُلَّةٌ (Thullah) is origin was used for groups of sheep, when the sheep are so numerous that you can’t count them. It refers to an uncountable large group.

Derivative: مَوْضُونَةٍ means to be woven and linked together in a precise and tight manner like armour producing a smooth result. It has also been interpreted to refer to beautified and adorned embroidery or decoration.
Derivative: مُّخَلَّدُونَ everlasting i.e., they will be kept at that age in an everlasting state.
Derivative: كَأْسٍ is a cup that is filled to the brim.
Derivative: أَبَارِيقَ are cups with handles or jugs.
Derivative: أَكْوَابٍ are large cups without handles.
Derivative: يُصَدَّعُونَ from صَدَّعَ – تَصْديع which means to be afflicted by a صُدَاع (headache).
Derivative: يُنْزِفُونَ which means to lose one’s mind i.e., due to intoxication.

لَيْسَ لِوَقْعَتِهَا كَاذِبَةٌ

- The word كَاذِبَةٌ here can be a صِفَة (adjective) here for a word that is not stated but can be implied or deduced like the word نَفْس (soul) making its meaning: “Its occurrence will not be denied by anyone or any soul”. Or it can be like a Masdar (verbal noun) with the meaning being: “There will be no denial of its occurrence”.
Derivative: يَتَخَيَّرُونَ from تَخَيَّرَ which is from اِخْتَارَ (to select/choose) and is the more hyperbolised form suggesting a vast selection to be chosen from.
Derivative: يَشْتَهُونَ from اِشْتَهَى – يَشْتَهِي which means what you desire according to your taste.
Derivative: عِينٌ can mean pleasing to the eyes or whose eyes are captivating. It has also been interpreted to mean eyes that are large in size.
Derivative: حُورٌ refers to women with eyes where the white of the eye is intensely white and the black of the eye is intensely black, with a stark contrast between the two colours.
Derivative: تَأْثِيمًا (Ta’theem) is what gets you into sin (إِثْم - Ithm) or leads to it.
Derivative: لَغْوًا refers to speech which is foolish, useless, nonsense, idle and improper. It is similar to what is known today as trash talk.

Derivative: مَخْضُودٍ – سِدْرٍ (a lote or lotus tree) is known to have a lot of thorns. مَخْضُودٍ means to be cut and trimmed whereby the thorns are removed. Its advantages and merits are maintained whilst all sources of harm are removed.
Derivative: مَنْضُودٍ from نَضَدَ means to be filled, layer upon layer, from top to bottom.
Derivative: طَلْحٍ is a name for another kind of tree, one interpretation is that it refers to a banana tree.

Derivative: خَافِضَةٌ from خَفَضَ – خَفْضُ which means to lower. خَافِضَةٌ refers to the event that will lower and رَافِعَةٌ is its opposite i.e., to raise or elevate. As such it is an event or day that lowers and elevates. In Arabic these terms are used both to refer to a place (i.e., in respect to being low or elevated) and also to standing and status (i.e., being lowered or elevated).
Derivative: مَّسْكُوبٍ to be flowing powerfully.
Derivative: مَقْطُوعَةٍ means to be cut off. Can mean separated from the tree and fallen to the ground. Or it can mean that they are in endless supply i.e., their supply is not cut off.

إِنَّا أَنْشَأْنَاهُنَّ إِنْشَاءً

- “We have raised them (i.e., women in Jannah) in a new creation” – It means that they have been created in a unique manner.
Derivative: عُرُبًا means to be devoted in affection and love (to their spouses), possessing the attributes that draw the spouse to her and make him love her more.
Derivative: أَتْرَابًا means of similar age and can also imply a similar intellectual level and compatibility. Possessing the compatible qualities of companionship.

Derivative: رُجَّتِ is the passive form of رَجَّ – رَجَّا which means to shake or convulse something with severity. رَجَّا is like the shaking of an earthquake or when something collides with a structure and shakes.
Derivative: سَمُومٍ A wind that is severe in its heat. سَمٌّ is Arabic is poison and this wind سَمُومٍ is a toxic burning poisonous one that makes you feel like you will die.
Derivative: يَحْمُومٍ is a black smoke that comes from fire.
Derivative: مُتْرَفِينَ – مُتْرَف comes from the word إِتْراف which means to be openly overindulgent and extravagant with wealth. Oblivious to how much one spends and consumes.
Derivative: الْحِنْثِ is the abominable sin and is in reference to the act of Shirk (Associating partners with Allah).
Derivative: بُسَّتِ is the passive form of بَسَّ – بَسَّا. It means to crush and break up into small fragments i.e., the mountains will be pulverised to a heap of sand, dust or small fragments.

Derivative: مَعْلُومٍ means to be known. Known here does not refer to the time of the appointment (i.e., Day of Judgement) but rather to the Day itself concerning which many names had been provided and its reality made evident.
Derivative: الضَّالُّونَ – ضالٌّ is someone who has lost his way and gone astray.

Derivative: الْهِيمِ – The Arabs say هَامَ – يَهِيمُ – هِيمًا which is used for a camel that gets lost and its water supply runs out. It roams aimlessly and then if it finds water it gulps from it as if its mad. It provides the image of the person in hell gulping from boiling scalding water like a desperate thirsty camel drinks water.
Derivative: نُزُلُ refers to the welcoming or introductory treatment, hospitality or appetiser like what a guest receives initially from the host when visiting him.
Derivative: تُمْنُونَ from أَمْنَى – يُمْنِي which means to emit fluid referring to the male’s sperm (مَنِيّ).
Derivative: قَدَّرْنَا from قَدَّرَ – تَقْدِير which means to calculate and determine.

Derivative: مَسْبُوقِينَ – مَسْبُوق is the اِسْم المَفْعُول (Ism ul-Maf’ool – Name of that which has been done to or object of action) from سَبَقَ whilst سَابِق is the اِسْمُ الفاعِل (Ism ul-Faa’il – Name of the doer). مَسْبُوق means to be preceded or beaten to something.

Derivative: حُطَامًا – Refers to broken or shattered fragments or debris that someone may step on without any concern. Here it refers to dried out crops that have crumbled, become worthless and have no benefit.

Derivative: تَفَكَّهُونَ – فَكِهَ means joyful whilst تَفَكَّهَ can mean both to be joyous and also the opposite i.e., to have all joy taken out. It is from the words in Arabic that can have opposite meanings from its root. Hence, the meaning of تَفَكَّهُونَ is to be filled with regret and remorse for what you had in the past and you will find no reason to be joyful.

Derivative: مُغْرَمُونَ is from أَغْرَمَ – إِغْرام which means to place a penalty on someone. مُغْرَم means to be penalised and can imply the feeling of being unfairly penalised and over burdened by it.
Derivative: الْمُزْنِ is the type of clouds filled and ready to burst with rain. It is the plural and the singular is مُزْنَة.

Derivative: أَزْوَاجًا is the plural of زَوْج which means a pair or spouse, or two parts of a whole, like the night and the day, the male and the female, the sun and the moon and the life of this world and the hereafter are all examples of pairs which complement each other. زَوْج is also used to refer to a group which is complementary and interdependent where it is incomplete without one of its members. Here it refers to three distinct categories or kinds of groups.

Derivative: لِلْمُقْوِينَ – From أَقْوَى - يُقْوِي which means to be in need and implies seeking something to meet that need. It is the image of travellers in the desert who find wood and make a fire to meet their needs.

Derivative: مَوَاقِعِ means the positions or locations (in reference to the stars). It originates from وَقَعَ which is to fall, in the case where something falls and stays in its place. مَوَاقِعِ are the places where something falls or can be located.

فَلَا أُقْسِمُ بِمَوَاقِعِ النُّجُومِ

- The purpose of an oath in Arabic is to grab the attention of the audience. The oath in the Qur’an however is not just used to grant attention but the object attached to the oath has a connection to and prepares the mind for what’s coming. Here the oath is being made by the stationary stars that the Arabs held in great significance and were most reliant upon when travelling.

وَإِنَّهُ لَقَسَمٌ لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عَظِيمٌ

- This is the only time in the Qur’an that Allah reiterates the oath after making it in the preceding verse.

Derivative: مَكْنُونٍ
- مَكْنُونٍ means to be concealed. Here in reference to the كِتاب (Kitaab) it implies protection and preservation. It is a hidden away treasured book, referring to Al-Lawh Al-Mahfoozh (The Preserved Tablet).

Derivative: الْمَيْمَنَةِ comes from اليَمين (Al-Yameen) which means the right hand as opposed to الشِّمال (Ash-Shimaal) which means the left hand. مَيْمَنَة (Maymanah) is what is known as a Masdar Meemiy (مَصْدَر مِيمي) and is stronger in import and indication than the normal Masdar of يَمين (Yameen). It implies that they are not only people of the right hand, but they are truly, fully and firmly from the people of the right hand.
Derivative: مُدْهِنُونَ from أَدْهَنَ – إِدْهَان and it means to be lenient, compromising and appeasing. To give and take.

Derivative: مَدِينِينَ – مَدين (Madeen) comes from the word دِين (Deen) which refers to a judgement that is passed. مَدين refers to the one whom a judgement will be passed against, recompensed and held to account i.e., on the Day of Judgement.
Derivative: رَيْحَانٌ is like اِسْتِراحَة (Istiraahah – relaxation) both being from رَاحَ. It means to be in state of relaxation.
Derivative: رَوْحٌ is another word that carries the meaning of رَحْمَة (care and mercy).

Derivative: The مَا (Maa) used here in مَا أَصْحَابُ الْمَشْأَمَةِ and also in the previous verse مَا أَصْحَابُ الْمَيْمَنَةِ is used for التَعَجُّب (At-Ta’ajjub) which implies emphasis or aggrandising.

Derivative: الْمَشْأَمَةِ means misfortune and it also used to mean the opposite of اليَمين (Al-Yameen) i.e., the left hand. مَشأَمَة comes from being unlucky, unfortunate and also to be wretched. From it the Arabic word for being pessimistic and negative is derived مُتَشَائِم (Mutashaa’im). Here, it can refer to those of the left hand and also those who are in a truly wretched condition.
